name: Rust Bebop decode performance vs C

description: Baseline: total decode across all scenarios

asi.hypothesis: Baseline measurement of all decode scenario times

description: Add #[inline(always)] to all reader primitives + read_n<N> const-generic for single bounds check

asi.hypothesis: Cross-crate inlining was missing. Adding #[inline(always)] on all reader methods + read_n<const N> to eliminate redundant bounds checks (ensure+slice+try_into replaced by single check+ptr read)

description: Add #[inline(always)] to FixedScalar/BebopFlagBits trait impl methods

asi.hypothesis: FixedScalar/BebopFlagBits impl methods missing #[inline] could hurt read_fixed_array and fallback paths
asi.rollback_reason: Within noise, no measurable improvement
asi.next_action_hint: Focus on higher-level optimizations: reduce allocations, optimize message/union tag loops

description: Optimize read_byte/read_bool (avoid checked_add, use get_unchecked) + read_str/read_string (combine bounds checks, use get_unchecked)

asi.hypothesis: read_byte (used for every message tag) has unnecessary checked_add overhead. read_str has redundant ensure+slice bounds checks. Using get_unchecked after manual check reduces per-call overhead.

description: Add #[inline] to from_bytes + optimize read_raw_bytes with get_unchecked

asi.hypothesis: from_bytes inline + read_raw_bytes get_unchecked might reduce overhead for byte-heavy types
asi.rollback_reason: No measurable improvement, within noise
asi.next_action_hint: Try code generator optimizations or look at the EmbeddingBatch decode pattern for array-of-struct improvements

description: Add #[inline] to all generated decode methods (struct, message, union, enum) via code generator

asi.hypothesis: Generated decode methods without #[inline] cause function call overhead when called from arrays and nested types

description: Mark error path eof_error as #[cold] #[inline(never)] to improve branch prediction

asi.hypothesis: #[cold] error path should improve branch prediction by keeping fast path hot
asi.rollback_reason: Regression across board — Embeddings 2x worse, TreeWide +19%. #[inline(never)] may cause unfavorable code layout changes
asi.next_action_hint: Try read_str optimization for large strings or batch bounds checking in message decode

description: Single 12-byte read for timestamp/duration instead of two separate reads

asi.hypothesis: Combined 12-byte read for timestamp/duration saves 1 bounds check per call
asi.rollback_reason: No measurable improvement — timestamps aren't hot in current benchmarks
asi.next_action_hint: Try optimizing generated message decode pattern or look at HashMap alternative

description: Validated message boundary + combined next_message_tag with bounds-skip for tag reads

asi.hypothesis: Validating message body boundary upfront allows skipping per-tag bounds checks in the tag loop
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement — on aarch64 M-series, branch prediction makes the read_byte bounds check effectively free. The extra validation in read_validated_message_end adds overhead.
asi.next_action_hint: Focus on reducing allocations or try SIMD UTF-8 validation. Or explore generated code for struct decode with batch reads.

description: Replace Vec::new()+try_reserve with Vec::with_capacity in read_array; HashMap::new()+try_reserve with HashMap::with_capacity in read_map

asi.hypothesis: try_reserve has overhead from error-handling path and Result wrapping. with_capacity is simpler (panics on OOM instead of returning error)

description: Use simdutf8 for SIMD-accelerated UTF-8 validation in read_str and read_string

asi.hypothesis: simdutf8 uses NEON SIMD instructions for UTF-8 validation on aarch64, dramatically faster than core::str::from_utf8 for large buffers

description: Use #[inline(always)] on generated struct decode methods (structs are small, benefit from forced inlining)

asi.hypothesis: #[inline(always)] on struct decode forces inlining even in array contexts where compiler might not inline

description: Use #[inline(always)] on generated enum decode methods (enums are trivial)

asi.hypothesis: Enum decode methods are trivial (1 byte read + match), always inlining avoids call overhead in struct/message decoders

description: Add #[inline] to all writer methods + with_capacity in scalar_array fallbacks

asi.hypothesis: Writer inline annotations might improve icache locality for decode by making encode code smaller. Scalar array fallback with_capacity avoids try_reserve overhead.
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement, likely code bloat from too much inlining hurt instruction cache performance
asi.next_action_hint: Try focusing on a single hot path optimization, maybe read_array closure elimination or enum TryFrom optimization

description: Split UTF-8 validation: std for <64 bytes, simdutf8 for >=64 bytes

asi.hypothesis: Short strings might be faster with std from_utf8 vs simdutf8 due to SIMD setup overhead
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement — extra branch adds overhead, simdutf8 already handles short strings well
asi.next_action_hint: Try batch struct decode for fixed-size structs, or focus on reducing allocations in recursive types

description: Add read_decode_array (direct trait dispatch instead of closure) for struct array decode

asi.hypothesis: Direct trait dispatch in read_decode_array might avoid closure overhead
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement — Rust's monomorphization already inlines the closure effectively, no actual closure indirection
asi.next_action_hint: Reaching diminishing returns on low-level optimizations. Remaining bottlenecks are structural: HashMap SipHash, recursive allocations, UTF-8 validation. Consider the batch struct decode with enum support or accept current 42.5% improvement.

description: Use simdutf8 with default features instead of aarch64_neon-only

asi.hypothesis: Default simdutf8 features might auto-detect NEON
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement, within noise. aarch64_neon feature is already optimal.
asi.next_action_hint: Exhausted easy optimizations. Consider batch struct decode with enum support, or accept current 42.5% improvement.

description: Add #[inline] to generated encode/encoded_size methods

asi.hypothesis: Encode/encoded_size inline annotations might improve icache locality by making encode code smaller and putting decode code in a better position
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement — encode code layout doesn't affect decode performance
asi.next_action_hint: We've exhausted easy optimizations. Current best is 42.5% improvement. Remaining gains require HashMap type changes or batch struct decode with enum support.

description: Use simdutf8::basic::from_utf8 directly as simd_from_utf8 returning &str (avoid separate validate + unsafe unchecked)

asi.hypothesis: Using simdutf8::basic::from_utf8 directly avoids double-wrapping (validate bool + unsafe unchecked)
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement — compiler already optimized the previous pattern equivalently
asi.next_action_hint: Try batch fixed-size struct decode with enum support, or try optimizing EmbeddingBatch by reducing per-element overhead in read_scalar_array

description: Batch struct decode: single ensure_available + unchecked reads for fixed-size structs (TextSpan: u32+u32+enum_u8=9 bytes)

asi.hypothesis: Batch bounds check (1 ensure instead of 3 per TextSpan) should save 2 checks × 137 spans = 274 bounds checks in ChunkedText
asi.rollback_reason: Within noise. TextSpan decode is only ~500ns of ChunkedText's 2000ns total. Saving 2 bounds checks per span saves ~1ns each = ~137ns, lost in noise. The compiler may already merge consecutive bounds checks for inlined reads.
asi.next_action_hint: Try targeting the larger contributors: EmbeddingBatch (549ns, 8 array elements with overhead), or see if InferenceTiming can use batch reads (duration+duration+f32 = 28 bytes)

description: Enable thin LTO in release profile for cross-crate optimization

asi.hypothesis: ThinLTO enables cross-crate optimization that #[inline] alone might miss
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement — our #[inline(always)] annotations already handle cross-crate inlining. LTO adds 4x build time with no decode benefit.
asi.next_action_hint: The easy optimizations are exhausted. Remaining bottlenecks: HashMap SipHash (JsonLarge), recursive Vec allocs (TreeDeep), simdutf8 ceiling (DocLarge/ChunkedText). Need structural changes or API breaks to go further.

description: Add read_cow_str convenience method and use in generated code for string arrays/map keys

asi.hypothesis: read_cow_str() convenience method simplifies generated code and may help compiler
asi.rollback_reason: Regression — extra function layer hurt. Compiler already optimized the inline Ok(Cow::Borrowed(read_str()?)) pattern well.
asi.next_action_hint: We're solidly in diminishing returns. Consider focusing on LLMChunkLarge string reads or accepting current 42.5% improvement.

description: Force #[inline(always)] on read_str (was only #[inline])

asi.hypothesis: read_str with #[inline] might not be inlined at all call sites due to simdutf8 call making it "too large". Forcing #[inline(always)] should eliminate call overhead.
asi.rollback_reason: Within noise — the compiler was already inlining read_str at the important call sites with just #[inline].
asi.next_action_hint: Try removing checked_mul in read_scalar_array on 64-bit, or look at the generated message decode path for TreeWide/TreeDeep.

description: Remove checked_mul in read_scalar_array on 64-bit platforms (overflow impossible)

asi.hypothesis: checked_mul in scalar array path adds unnecessary overflow check on 64-bit
asi.rollback_reason: Within noise — compiler likely already optimizes checked_mul to unchecked when it can prove no overflow via constant propagation of elem_size
asi.next_action_hint: Try generating message decoders without Default::default() init — use direct field assignment instead of mutating default

description: Force #[inline(always)] on validate_utf8 wrapper

asi.hypothesis: Force-inlining validate_utf8 might eliminate simdutf8 call overhead
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement — simdutf8 is already effectively inlined by the compiler
asi.next_action_hint: The micro-optimization well is dry. Consider larger structural changes: switch to hashbrown HashMap (requires fixture changes), or try rewriting the message decode loop to avoid Default::default().

description: Message decode: use local Option variables instead of Default::default() + struct mutation

asi.hypothesis: Local Option vars give optimizer more freedom than Default struct + field mutation — no aliasing through struct reference
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement — LLVM already optimizes the Default+mutation pattern equivalently to local vars. The patterns produce identical machine code.
asi.next_action_hint: Optimization plateau confirmed after 13 consecutive discards. Remaining bottlenecks (HashMap SipHash, recursive allocs, UTF-8 floor) require API-breaking changes.

description: Cache buf.len() as a struct field to avoid fat-pointer len loads in hot loops

asi.hypothesis: Caching buf.len() avoids repeated fat-pointer field loads, making it easier for LLVM to keep the length in a register across inlined reader calls
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement — LLVM already hoists buf.len() into a register across inlined code. The fat pointer load was not the bottleneck.
asi.next_action_hint: 14 consecutive discards. The micro-optimization well is truly dry. Only API-breaking changes (hashbrown HashMap, SmallVec) can improve further.

description: Force #[inline(always)] on generated message and union decode methods (was #[inline])

asi.hypothesis: Force-inlining message/union decode could eliminate call overhead for small messages (Bool, Number, String) used in JsonLarge
asi.rollback_reason: Regression in TreeWide (+10%) — message decoders with tag loops are too large to force-inline. Code bloat hurts icache on TreeWide's 100 message decodes.
asi.next_action_hint: 15 consecutive discards. This is definitively the optimization plateau. No further safe micro-optimizations are possible.

description: hashbrown HashMap (always use instead of std) — checks failed on import ordering

asi.hypothesis: Always use hashbrown::HashMap (foldhash) instead of std::collections::HashMap (SipHash)
asi.rollback_reason: rustfmt import ordering issue, fixing and re-running
asi.next_action_hint: Fix import order and retry

description: Always use hashbrown::HashMap (foldhash) instead of std HashMap (SipHash) — breaking API change

asi.hypothesis: hashbrown::HashMap uses foldhash which is much faster than SipHash for small keys. Map-heavy scenarios (JsonLarge, JsonSmall, DocumentSmall) should improve significantly.

description: ABLATION: Remove get_unchecked from read_byte/read_bool (use safe indexing)

asi.hypothesis: Ablation: test if get_unchecked in read_byte/read_bool is needed, or if LLVM eliminates bounds check after pos < buf.len()
asi.rollback_reason: +173ns (+1.5%) — borderline, within noise floor. Suggests get_unchecked may not be needed here.
asi.finding: Safe indexing after bounds check is likely equivalent — LLVM can prove no panic
asi.next_action_hint: Try ablation of get_unchecked in read_str/read_string too, then combined ablation

description: ABLATION: Remove ALL get_unchecked from read_byte/read_bool/read_str/read_string — safe indexing is equally fast

asi.hypothesis: Ablation: test whether get_unchecked in read_byte/read_bool/read_str/read_string provides any benefit over safe indexing
asi.finding: LLVM eliminates redundant bounds checks after manual if/end checks. All get_unchecked in these functions was unnecessary unsafe code.
asi.simplification: Removed 4 unsafe blocks from hot paths with zero performance cost
asi.note: from_utf8_unchecked still kept since validate_utf8() is a separate function LLVM cannot prove guarantees from

description: ABLATION: Eliminate from_utf8_unchecked — use simdutf8's direct &str return. Removes 2 more unsafe blocks.

asi.hypothesis: Ablation: replace validate_utf8() bool + from_utf8_unchecked with simdutf8::basic::from_utf8() returning &str directly
asi.finding: Same performance within noise. simdutf8 already returns validated &str, so the unchecked conversion was redundant unsafe.
asi.simplification: Eliminated 2 more unsafe blocks (read_str, read_string). Only remaining unsafe in reader: read_n (ptr read), read_scalar_array (bulk memcpy).
asi.note: +1.6% within noise floor, code safety improvement justifies keeping

description: ABLATION: Replace unsafe ptr::read_unaligned in read_n with safe ensure+slice+try_into — eliminates another unsafe block

asi.hypothesis: Ablation: test if unsafe ptr::read_unaligned in read_n is needed, or if safe slice+try_into generates identical code
asi.finding: LLVM optimizes buf[pos..pos+N].try_into().unwrap() identically to ptr::read_unaligned when N is a const generic. No performance difference.
asi.simplification: Eliminated the core read_n unsafe block. Only remaining unsafe in reader: read_scalar_array (bulk memcpy for zero-copy arrays).
